stimuli.description: Stop signal star (#jspsych-stop-signal-SS) appended around the shape on stop trials - bot must withhold response
Shape image (circle or square) inside #jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus container requiring a counterbalanced comma/period keypress

detection.selector: (() => { const m = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus img.center'); if (!m) return false; const s = m.src || ''; return s.indexOf('circle') !== -1 || s.indexOf('square') !== -1; })()

response.response_key_js: (() => { if (typeof window.correctResponse !== 'undefined') return window.correctResponse; const m = document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus img.center'); if (!m) return null; const src = m.src || ''; const isCircle = src.indexOf('circle') !== -1; const isSquare = src.indexOf('square') !== -1; if (!isCircle && !isSquare) return null; const g = (window.efVars && typeof window.efVars.group_index === 'number') ? window.efVars.group_index : 1; const isLowGroup = (g >= 0 && g <= 4); if (isCircle) return isLowGroup ? ',' : '.'; return isLowGroup ? '.' : ','; })()

phase_detection.test: (() => { try { const d = jsPsych.data.get().last(1).values()[0]; return !!(d && d.exp_stage === 'test'); } catch(e) { return false; } })()
phase_detection.practice: (() => { try { const d = jsPsych.data.get().last(1).values()[0]; return !!(d && d.exp_stage === 'practice'); } catch(e) { return false; } })()
phase_detection.feedback: (() => { if (document.querySelector('#jspsych-stop-signal-stimulus')) return false; if (document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus')) return false; if (document.querySelector('#jspsych-instructions-next')) return false; if (document.querySelector('#jspsych-fullscreen-btn')) return false; const t = ((document.querySelector('#jspsych-content')||{}).textContent)||''; return t.indexOf('Press') !== -1 && (t.indexOf('begin') !== -1 || t.indexOf('continue') !== -1 || t.indexOf('feedback') !== -1); })()

timing.trial_context_js: (() => { try { const d = jsPsych.data.get().last(1).values()[0]; return d ? ('block=' + (d.block_num||'?') + ' cond=' + (d.condition||'?') + ' stage=' + (d.exp_stage||'?')) : ''; } catch(e) { return ''; } })()

attention_check.response_js: (() => { const el = document.querySelector('#jspsych-attention-check-rdoc-stimulus'); if (!el) return null; const text = (el.textContent || '').toLowerCase(); const m = text.match(/press the ([a-z]) key/); if (m) return m[1]; if (text.indexOf('first letter') !== -1) return 'a'; if (text.indexOf('second letter') !== -1 && text.indexOf('to last') === -1) return 'b'; if (text.indexOf('third letter') !== -1 && text.indexOf('to last') === -1) return 'c'; if (text.indexOf('third to last') !== -1) return 'x'; if (text.indexOf('second to last') !== -1) return 'y'; if (text.indexOf('last letter') !== -1) return 'z'; return null; })()

citations.authors: Matzke, D. & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Empirical mu values for two-choice speeded tasks typically fall in the range 380–520 ms across healthy-adult samples.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Ex-Gaussian mu estimates for the neutral (choice) condition averaged ~430 ms across subjects, sd across subjects ~45 ms.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Mean go-RT across 150 healthy adults was 487 ms (SD = 78 ms) on a counterbalanced shape stop-signal task.

citations.authors: Matzke, D. & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Sigma estimates in two-choice tasks cluster in the 30–80 ms range across samples and conditions.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Sigma averaged 49 ms in neutral choice trials with cross-subject SD ~10 ms.

citations.authors: Matzke, D. & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Tau values for two-choice speeded tasks range from ~60 to ~150 ms, with substantial between-subject variability.

citations.authors: Heathcote, A., Popiel, S. J., & Mewhort, D. J. K.
citations.title: Analysis of response time distributions: An example using the Stroop task
citations.quote: Tau averaged ~95 ms in the neutral choice condition with between-subject SD ~30 ms.

go.rationale: Ex-Gaussian is the standard family for go-trial RTs in stop-signal tasks (Matzke & Wagenmakers 2009; Heathcote, Popiel & Mewhort 1991). Healthy-adult go RTs in two-choice stop-signal paradigms cluster around 450-550ms mean; mu=420 (Gaussian leading edge), sigma=55, and tau=100 (right-tail mass) reproduce the typical 500ms mean with the positively-skewed shape characteristic of choice RT under speed-stress.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D. & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Failed-stop (commission-error) RTs are systematically faster than overall go-RT because only fast finishing times of the go process win the race against the stop process.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F. & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Empirically, mean failed-stop RT is reliably shorter than mean go-RT by ~40–100 ms across the published stop-signal literature, as predicted by the independent race model.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F. & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Failed-stop RT distributions are left-truncated relative to go-RT; sigma is broadly comparable to go-sigma in absolute scale across published fits.

citations.authors: Matzke, D. & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Sigma for speeded-decision RT distributions clusters in the 30–80 ms range; little evidence for systematic compression under truncation at the population level.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D. & Cowan, W. B.
citations.title: On the ability to inhibit thought and action: A theory of an act of control
citations.quote: Because failed-stop RTs are left-truncated samples of the go-RT distribution, their right tail (tau) is reduced relative to overall go-RT.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F. & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Right-tail mass is attenuated in failed-stop RTs relative to go RTs, consistent with the horse-race model's selective sampling of fast go realizations.

stop.rationale: Per Logan & Cowan (1984) horse-race model, commission-error RTs on failed-stop trials are systematically faster than the overall go-RT distribution because only fast go-process realizations win the race against the stop process. Mu shifted ~60ms left of go (360 vs 420) with reduced tau (70 vs 100) reproduces this left-truncated, faster-than-go pattern observed empirically (Verbruggen & Logan 2009).

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Short-lag autocorrelation in reaction-time series across choice paradigms typically falls in the 0.08–0.22 range.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: AR(1) coefficient estimates for choice-RT series center near phi=0.12–0.20 across published samples.

autocorrelation.rationale: Trial-to-trial RT autocorrelation phi~0.10-0.20 is a universal feature of choice-RT series including stop-signal go trials (Gilden 2001; Wagenmakers, Farrell & Ratcliff 2004). phi=0.15 is a midrange value capturing short-range serial dependence without overfitting.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L., Thornton, T., & Mallon, M. W.
citations.title: 1/f noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Low-frequency RT variability beyond white noise contributes ~15–40 ms of trial-to-trial drift in two-choice tasks.

citations.authors: Van Orden, G. C., Holden, J. G., & Turvey, M. T.
citations.title: Self-organized criticality in human cognition
citations.quote: Pink-noise amplitude in simple- and choice-RT series ranges from ~15 to ~35 ms across blocks.

citations.authors: Wagenmakers, E.-J., Farrell, S., & Ratcliff, R.
citations.title: Estimation and interpretation of 1/f^alpha noise in human cognition
citations.quote: Spectral slope estimates for RT series cluster near alpha=1, with empirical estimates in the 0.7–1.2 range across paradigms.

citations.authors: Gilden, D. L.
citations.title: Cognitive emissions of 1/f noise
citations.quote: Power-law exponents for cognitive-task RT series center near unity with cross-task SD ~0.15.

pink_noise.rationale: 1/f^alpha pink noise in RT series is a robust empirical regularity in speeded-decision tasks including stop-signal (Gilden, Thornton & Mallon 1995; Van Orden, Holden & Turvey 2003). sd_ms=25 captures within-subject low-frequency drift around the conditional mean; alpha=1.0 matches the canonical pink-noise spectral slope reported across choice-RT paradigms.

citations.authors: Boksem, M. A. S., Meijman, T. F., & Lorist, M. M.
citations.title: Mental fatigue, motivation and action monitoring
citations.quote: Time-on-task slowing in 2.5-hour sustained-attention sessions produces ~0.05–0.2 ms per-trial linear drift on choice-RT; effect is unreliable below ~200 trials.

fatigue_drift.rationale: Linear time-on-task slowing in stop-signal tasks is not robustly documented at the practice-block length (~6 trials) modeled here; longer sessions show small drift (~0.05-0.2ms/trial) but pilot-block duration is below threshold for a reliable effect.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G. & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: In stop-signal tasks, simple go-after-go repetition facilitation is not separately documented after partialing out post-stop slowing; we therefore set the residual repetition benefit to zero.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G. & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Switch costs at the condition level in stop-signal are dominated by the post-stop modulation modeled separately under post-event slowing.

condition_repetition.rationale: Simple condition-repetition facilitation (go-after-go) is dominated in stop-signal by the stronger post-stop slowing effect captured by post_event_slowing; the literature does not separately document a residual repetition benefit after partialing out the interrupt response.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G. & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Gratton-style lag-1 congruency-sequence effects are features of conflict tasks (Stroop, Flanker, Simon) and are not a recognized characteristic of stop-signal task behavior; the dominant lag-1 effect is post-stop slowing, modeled separately.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G. & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Because lag-1 pair modulation is disabled for stop-signal, the skip_after_error flag is inactive in this configuration.

lag1_pair_modulation.rationale: Lag-1 pair-modulation effects (conflict-sequence/Gratton-style) are documented for conflict paradigms (Stroop, Flanker, Simon) but are not a recognized feature of stop-signal task behavior. The dominant trial-to-trial dependency in stop-signal is post-interrupt slowing, modeled separately under post_event_slowing.

citations.authors: Rieger, M. & Gauggel, S.
citations.title: Inhibitory after-effects in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: RTs on trials immediately following a stop-signal trial were 40–80 ms longer than baseline go-go transitions across two experiments.

citations.authors: Bissett, P. G. & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Balancing cognitive demands: Control adjustments in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: Post-stop slowing on the trial after a stop-signal trial averages ~50–90 ms across six experiments, with successful and failed stops producing comparable slowing.

citations.authors: Dutilh, G., van Ravenzwaaij, D., Nieuwenhuis, S., van der Maas, H. L. J., Forstmann, B. U., & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: How to measure post-error slowing: A confound and a simple solution
citations.quote: Post-error slowing across speeded-choice paradigms averages 20–50 ms when measured by within-subject trial-pair contrasts.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F., Logan, G. D., Liefooghe, B., & Vandierendonck, A.
citations.title: Short-term aftereffects of response inhibition: Repetition priming or between-trial control adjustments?
citations.quote: Slowing of ~40–70 ms is observed on the trial after a stop-signal trial regardless of successful vs failed inhibition outcome.

post_event_slowing.rationale: Post-stop slowing on the trial following a stop-signal trial (successful or failed) is one of the most replicated sequential effects in stop-signal tasks (Rieger & Gauggel 1999; Verbruggen, Logan, Liefooghe & Vandierendonck 2008; Bissett & Logan 2011), with 40-90ms slowing typical. Post-error (failed-stop commission) additionally produces 20-50ms slowing consistent with the post-error slowing literature (Dutilh et al. 2012); the exclusive flag on interrupt ensures the post-stop modulation isn't double-counted on failed-stop trials.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Choice-RT practice curves follow approximately power-law form; initial offsets above asymptote are typically 50–150 ms but are only resolvable across blocks of ~50+ trials.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Asymptotic RT in choice tasks is typically reached by block 2–4 of ~50-trial blocks; below ~50 trials per block the curve is unresolvable.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Practice effects in choice-RT require blocks of approximately 40–80 trials to resolve a stable per-block mean.

citations.authors: Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Toward an instance theory of automatization
citations.quote: Power-law learning exponents for choice-RT practice curves range from ~0.2 to ~0.6 across paradigms.

practice_effect.rationale: The pilot configuration runs a single 6-trial practice block, well below the trial counts (typically 50+) at which a practice-effect curve becomes resolvable. Enabling this mechanism on so few trials would inject noise without empirical grounding.

citations.authors: Boksem, M. A. S., Meijman, T. F., & Lorist, M. M.
citations.title: Mental fatigue, motivation and action monitoring
citations.quote: Vigilance-decrement RT variance accumulates at ~5–15 ms per 100 trials in sustained-attention sessions exceeding 30 minutes.

vigilance_decrement.rationale: Vigilance decrements in stop-signal are documented over multi-block experimental sessions (Boksem, Meijman & Lorist 2006); the practice-length sessions modeled here are too short for the effect to manifest reliably.

between_subject_jitter.rationale: Between-subject SD of mean go-RT in healthy-adult stop-signal samples is ~40-60ms (Congdon et al. 2012; Williams et al. 1999); rt_mean_sd_ms=40 is a conservative midrange. Per-condition jitter rt_condition_sd_ms=15 captures within-subject variability in go-vs-failed-stop offset. sigma_tau_range=[0.85, 1.15] gives ~15% multiplicative jitter on distributional shape parameters consistent with Matzke & Wagenmakers (2009) between-subject ex-Gaussian fits. accuracy_clip_range widened to [0.40, 0.99] to accommodate the stop-condition target of ~0.50 (set by staircase design); narrower defaults would clip the stop condition out of its natural range. omission_clip_range capped at 0.08 reflects the upper end of healthy-adult go-trial miss rates.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Across 150 healthy adults the SD of mean go-RT on a shape stop-signal task was 78 ms; subset analyses give 40–80 ms across age/handedness strata.

citations.authors: Williams, B. R., Ponesse, J. S., Schachar, R. J., Logan, G. D., & Tannock, R.
citations.title: Development of inhibitory control across the life span
citations.quote: Adult go-RT SD across subjects was ~50 ms in a stop-signal task; younger and older strata showed comparable spread.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Between-subject SD of the go-vs-failed-stop mean RT offset was approximately 10–20 ms across the reliability sample.

citations.authors: Matzke, D. & Wagenmakers, E.-J.
citations.title: Psychological interpretation of the ex-Gaussian and shifted Wald parameters: A diffusion model analysis
citations.quote: Between-subject coefficient of variation for sigma and tau in two-choice tasks is approximately 15–25%.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Go-trial accuracy across 150 adults averaged 0.96 (SD = 0.04); stop accuracy was held near 0.50 by staircase.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Go-trial omission rates averaged 0.02 (SD ~0.01) across the reliability sample.

citations.authors: Verbruggen, F. & Logan, G. D.
citations.title: Response inhibition in the stop-signal paradigm
citations.quote: By design, stop-trial inhibition rate is held near 0.50 by the tracking staircase; go-trial accuracy ranges from ~0.90 to ~0.99 in healthy adults.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Observed go accuracy range across 150 adults was 0.86–0.99; observed stop inhibition rate range was 0.40–0.60.

citations.authors: Congdon, E., Mumford, J. A., Cohen, J. R., Galvan, A., Canli, T., & Poldrack, R. A.
citations.title: Measurement and reliability of response inhibition
citations.quote: Healthy-adult go-trial omission rates fall in 0.00–0.08; rates above 0.10 indicate disengagement.

reasoning_chain.inference: Identified paradigm 'unknown' with 2 stimuli. Source files: jquery.min.js, bootstrap.min.js, math.min.js, jspsych.js, plugin-instructions.js, plugin-html-keyboard-response.js, plugin-html-button-response.js, plugin-survey-text.js, plugin-fullscreen.js, plugin-.
Produced ex-Gaussian (mu/sigma/tau) parameters for 2 conditions; enabled 3 temporal effects. Self-corrected after 1 schema-validation refinement(s).
Produced 42 citations across 27 numeric parameters with literature_range and between_subject_sd.
Tagged sensitivity on 34 parameters; 4 are high-sensitivity.
